title: Laser Classification System
description: Understanding Class 1 through Class 4 laser classifications and what each means for safety requirements.

phases.title: Classification Preview
phases.content: Laser classification is the foundation of laser safety. Each class has specific hazard characteristics and corresponding control requirements. Think about how you would categorize different types of lasers you've encountered.

phases.title: Understanding Laser Classes
phases.content:
The laser classification system categorizes lasers based on their accessible emission limits (AEL) and the hazards they present.

## Class 1: Inherently Safe
- Safe under all normal operating conditions
- May contain Class 3B or 4 lasers fully enclosed
- No hazard during normal use

## Class 2: Low Power Visible
- Visible lasers (400-700nm) up to 1mW
- Blink reflex provides adequate protection
- Hazardous only with intentional prolonged viewing

## Class 3R: Reduced Risk
- Visible lasers up to 5x Class 2 AEL
- Low risk normally safe with aversion response
- Direct viewing potentially hazardous

## Class 3B: Direct Viewing Hazard
- Continuous wave up to 0.5W
- Direct beam viewing always hazardous
- Diffuse reflections generally safe

## Class 4: High Power
- All lasers above Class 3B limits
- Eye and skin hazard from direct and diffused reflections
- Fire and ignition hazard
- Comprehensive controls required

phases.title: Classification Practice
phases.content:
Classify the following laser systems:

1. 5mW red laser pointer used for presentations
2. 10W RGB laser projector for entertainment
3. Enclosed CD player laser
4. 500mW CW laboratory laser at 532nm
5. 50W laser cutting system

keyTakeaways: Class 1: Safe under all normal conditions — inherently safe or fully enclosed
Class 2: Low power visible lasers — blink reflex provides adequate protection
Class 3R/3B: Medium power — direct viewing hazardous without controls
Class 4: High power — hazardous to eye and skin, diffuse reflections dangerous
